data.title: Procter & Gamble: No Major Incentive To Turn Bullish Before Q1 Earnings
data.description:
Introduction

Procter & Gamble (NYSE:PG), one of the prominent consumer goods entities in the world, has seen its stock perform rather well this year; all through 2024, it has managed to consistently stay ahead of other staple peers from the S&P500 (on average), and as things stand, is up by a healthy 20% on YTD basis.

In a couple of days, the PG stock will encounter a catalyst that could be instrumental in facilitating even further outperformance (or vice versa, like the previous quarter, where the share ended up gapping down and falling lower by 7%). The catalyst in question is the Q1-25 earnings event (PG follows a June ending fiscal) which is due to take place on the 18th of October before market hours. In this article, we'll touch upon some of the key sub-plots that could dominate the event.

Earnings- What To Keep An Eye On?

Stakeholders of PG may likely be experiencing some degree of trepidation, as the company’s performance during earnings season has been quite mixed in recent periods. On the bottom-line front, there hasn’t really been a problem. For context, over the last 3 years, it has missed quarterly consensus EPS numbers just once (the June 2022 quarter), and just about met street estimates once (the December 2022 quarter). However, on all the other 10 instances, it has managed to beat street estimates, and on average, you’re looking at a business that facilitates earnings beat to the tune of +3% every quarter.

It's the top line where things appear to be dicey, as PG has now wobbled in each of the last 3 quarters. In Q2-24, the topline miss came in only at -0.3%, but in Q3 and Q4-24, the magnitude of topline attrition (relative to consensus numbers) has exceeded the 1% mark.

For the upcoming Q1, the two key headline numbers to watch are an EPS of $1.90 and a revenue figure of $22.01bn.

Keen scrutiny will fall on PG’s organic sales growth, as this was a big miss in the previous quarter (delivered only 2% growth whereas consensus was budgeting for +3.4%), and was instrumental in sending the share price lower. Separately note that it has been trending lower for 4 straight quarters now, so if PG can show signs of arresting this trend, it should be taken well by the market. However, it won’t be easy to facilitate, as last year’s base effect (Q1-24) too was quite high at 7%. Even if we don’t quite see an uplift in Q1, better trends are expected in the quarters ahead, as PG expects to close next year having delivered organic sales growth of +4%

PG is also poorly positioned as its largest product segment- Fabric care (24% of group sales, twice as much as the next largest contributor-Home Care), is currently not generating any organic growth, and globally it lost share by 1% last quarter. The European component of fabric care is unlikely to stage any dramatic growth numbers any time soon, as it is up against difficult comparables from last year. However, we would be looking for signs for the North American business to show some signs of life (despite higher promotions) as PG had launched a new innovation bundle (for instance, combining high penetration products with low penetration products such as liquid detergent and fabric enhancers) in the Spring season.

What could also prove to be a drag in Q3 is the Chinese Fabric portfolio where PG is in the midst of undertaking portfolio rationalization decisions (focusing only on the profitable part of the business and trimming the rest) that will have an adverse short-term impact but could work well in the long-run.

Speaking of China, this is a key terrain not just for Fabric, but the overall PG business, as it is the second-largest business for them. PG does deserve some credit for holding share in both their online and offline businesses (excluding the impact of high-end skincare brand SK-II on account of the Japanese linkage) there when consumer confidence had almost dipped to record lows last month. The jury is still out on whether the government’s fiscal measures will be sufficient to stimulate consumer momentum, but if PG management can provide positive granular details on a pickup in trends here, it should be taken well by the market. The ongoing drop-off in SK-II sales, a super-premium brand with strong ASPs (average selling prices) has played a part in the ’mix’ facet being only a neutral contributor to PG’s organic sales growth, but in September, PG management highlighted how consumers were now becoming more receptive to the revamped version of the brand again.

Despite a weak mix effect, note that PG’s gross margins (GM) are at decade highs now, and in the last quarter grew by 120bps. We would expect GM progress to persist in Q1-25, as PG still has a long-term goal of extracting$1.5bn worth of pre-tax gross savings from the cost of goods line due to productivity initiatives.

PG’s free cash performance will also receive plenty of attention too, as it serves as the foundation of the company’s stellar dividend profile. Note that at the current share price, the PG’s shares’ FCF yield is already below its long-term average. One key metric to watch here will be the adjusted free cash flow productivity ratio which essentially shows how effectively PG is converting its adjusted net earnings to FCF.

Investors shouldn’t expect the moon here in Q1-25, as Q4 already saw an inordinately high figure of 148% in Q4 which certainly won’t be easy to replicate again. Besides, after coming in at 105% for the whole year, the expected adjusted FCF productivity for FY25 is likely to be around the 90% mark.

What Does The Medium-term Outlook and Forward Valuations Look Like?

PG’s forward valuations don’t look overly prohibitive; based on the June 2026 EPS of 7.42, the stock is currently priced at P/E of 23.4x which is roughly in line with the stock’s long-term rolling average of around 23x.

If you want to juxtapose the quantum of medium-term earnings growth (single-digit earnings growth) with the double-digit P/E multiple of PG, we acknowledge, the stock may not come across as a beacon of value, but we’d ask investors to view things through a different lens.

Between FY24 and FY27, PG’s topline growth p.a. is expected to improve for three straight years, but the pace of growth on the bottom line too will lead the topline growth by a much superior margin in each of those years. To facilitate this degree of operating leverage in 1 year is hard enough, but to likely do it 3 years running is a feat not too many staple companies can boast off.

Closing Thoughts- What Do The Charts Suggest?

Based on what the charts are suggesting, we don’t believe it makes a great deal of sense to load up on PG now.

The dividend is an important component of PG’s story, as it has been doing it out for 134 years, and growing it for 68 years. The most recent hike in dividends was also quite compelling at 7% (the previous hike was only 3%), but despite the hefty hike, note that such has been the appreciation in the share price, that the yield at current levels is quite sub-par at only 2.24%, which is 16bps lower than its long-term average.

Meanwhile, investors looking out for suitable mean-reversion opportunities within the large-cap consumer staple universe are unlikely to gravitate to the Procter & Gamble stock now. The prospect looks unlikely as PG’s relative strength (RS) ratio vs an equally weighted basket of staple stocks has more than mean-reverted and is now trading 13% more than its long-term average.

Finally, also take a look at PG’s long-term monthly chart where the price action is showing signs of fatigue. Since the stock bottomed out in Oct 2022, the price has trended up in the shape of a rising wedge pattern (marked by the two converging black lines). The rising wedge pattern is perceived to be a reversal pattern, and even if we don’t necessarily see a monumental shift in sentiment, do consider that the price looks quite stretched as it is hugging the upper boundary of the wedge. Last month’s close was also concerning as it represented a shooting star candle (highlighted in yellow) at the upper Bollinger band boundary, which already represents a degree of overextension as it is 2 standard deviations away from the 20-period moving average.

To close, we feel a hold rating on PG feels fitting now.

data.title: D.R. Horton: Take Part In The Real Estate Boom, Upon A Correction
data.description:
DHI Remains Attractively Valued For The Upcoming Real Estate Boom, After A Moderate Correction

We previously covered D.R. Horton, Inc. (NYSE:DHI) in March 2024, discussing why we had maintained our Buy rating, attributed to the robust sales and performance metrics compared to pre-pandemic levels.

This was on top of the healthier balance sheet and secure dividend investment thesis, with it allowing opportunistic investors to take advantage of the recent pullback and the reasonably priced stock.

DHI YTD Stock Price

Since then, DHI has already rallied by +19.5%, compared to the wider market at +13.2% - with the same exuberance also observed in its US homebuilding peers.

Much of the recovery is attributed to the stock market rotation from high growth to value/ dividend stocks in July 2024, significantly aided by the Fed's recent pivot by 50 basis points in the September 2024 FOMC meeting.

The latter has already contributed to the moderating 30Y Fixed Rate Mortgage Average to 6.32% and the 15Y rate to 5.41% by October 10, 2024, down drastically from the peak of 7.79% and 7.03% observed on October 26, 2023, respectively.

As a result of the lower borrowing costs, it is unsurprising that Redfin (RDFN), the online real estate brokerage company, has already reported a sudden increase in mortgage-rate locks by +68% MoM by September 23, 2024.

This is on top of RDFN's Homebuyer Demand Index (a measure of tours and other buying services from Redfin agents) rising drastically to nearly "its highest level since May (2024)" as of October 10, 2024 - with mortgage-purchase applications also going up by +8% MoM, building upon the +10% MoM expansion observed by September 23, 2024.

These developments matter indeed, since the lower borrowing costs may trigger higher home buying activities, with it already contributing to the DHI stock's robust performance on a YTD basis.

At the same time, the median sales price gap between existing homes and new houses has narrowed drastically to only ~1% at between $416.7K and $420.6K by August 2024, respectively, compared to 30.8% observed in December 2022 and 20% in December 2019.

Readers must also note that DHI has reported an average selling price of approximately $380K per home in the latest earnings call, with it underscoring its near-term tailwinds as the largest homebuilding company in the US.

As a result of these promising market trends and the nearing of "magic mortgage rates of 5%," we can understand why the management has raised their FY2024 revenue guidance to $37B at the midpoint (+4.2% YoY) and home closings to 90.25K (+7.34K YoY).

This is up from the original guidance of $36.5B (+2.8% YoY) and 87.5K (+4.59K YoY) offered in the FQ4'23 earnings call, respectively.

If recent market developments are of any indication, we may see DHI report another double beat performance in the upcoming FQ4'24 earnings call scheduled on October 29, 2024, building upon the historical consecutive beats over the past 7 quarters (barring a one-time minimal adj EPS miss in FQ1'24).

This is especially since the homebuilder has 8.8K unsold completed homes and 17.4K in progress homes at highly favorable price points as of the latest quarter, allowing them to partake in the upcoming real estate boom.

The same has been hinted by the DHI management, based on the FQ4'24 home sales gross margin guidance of 24% (inline QoQ/ -1 YoY) compared to FQ3'23 levels of 24% (+0.9 points QoQ/ +0.7 YoY), YTD levels of 23.3% (+0.4 points YoY), and FY2019 levels of 20.3% (-1.1 points YoY).

This is on top of the 24.25K homes sales expected in FQ4'24 (+5.4% QoQ/ +5.8% YoY) - with the home builder likely to offer a similarly robust FY2025 sales guidance as the Fed continues on their rate normalization path.

Readers cannot forget DHI's well diversified operations across rental homes, mortgage financing, title services, and insurance agency services for its homebuyers as well.

The rate cut may allow the homebuilder to report higher (and less risky) loan originations, significantly aided by the stronger FICO scores at 725 and the higher buyer incomes at approximately $100K as of July 2024.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

As a result, we can understand why the consensus has expected DHI to record a robust FY2025 and FY2026 sales performance at high single digits, albeit still decelerating from its 5Y pre-pandemic top/ bottom-line expansion at a CAGR of +17%/ +21.9% and its 10Y normalized averages of +18.9%/ +26.4% (partly aided by acquisitions), respectively.

Despite the growth moderation, we believe that dividend oriented investors need not fret, since the homebuilder remains more than well capitalized to return great value to existing shareholders.

This is attributed to the management's FY2024 cash flow guidance of $3B (-30.2% YoY/ +237% from FY2019 levels of $892M), compared to the LTM dividend obligations of $381.8M (+13.9% sequentially) and LTM share repurchases of $1.73B (+60.1% sequentially), while building upon the CEO's highly promising guidance:

But we do expect that as cash flow does provide a significant increase next year (FY2025), we will increase our repurchases and dividends proportionately to that. (Seeking Alpha)

DHI Valuations

And this is also why we believe that DHI does not appear to be overly expensive at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 12.57x, compared to its 5Y mean of 9.13x and 10Y mean of 10.53x.

This is attributed to its reasonably valued PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.91x compared to the sector median of 1.54x and its direct peers, including Lennar Corporation (LEN) at FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.80x and PulteGroup, Inc. (PHM) at 0.56x, with it offering interested investors with a decent margin of safety.

So, Is DHI Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

DHI 5Y Stock Price

For now, DHI has already run away from its 100/ 200 day moving averages, while charting new peaks in September 2024.

Based on the LTM adj EPS of $14.89 ending FQ3'24 and the 10Y P/E non-GAAP mean of 10.53x, it appears that the stock has run away from our updated fair value estimates of $156.80.

Based on the consensus FY2026 adj EPS estimates of $17.69, there remains a minimal margin of safety to our long-term price target of $186.20 as well - with it apparent that the market has gotten over exuberant now.

The recent rally has also triggered DHI's lower forward yields of 0.66%, compared to its 5Y mean of 1.02% and the sector median of 2.25%, with it triggering a relatively underwhelming dividend investment thesis.

If anything, with the stock currently retesting the Q3'24 support levels of $180s after failing to break out of the all-time heights of $197s, we believe that it is likely to further pull back to its August 2024 support levels of $160s - with those levels also nearer to our fair value estimates.

As a result, while we are maintaining our Buy rating for the DHI stock, it is with the caveat that interested investors wait for the retracement to occur and only add at $160s for an improved margin of safety.

We believe that a market wide correction is bound to happen as the stock market enters the uncertain Q3'24 earnings season while boasting an increasingly greedy market sentiments and elevated CBOE Volatility Index.

Patience may be more prudent in the meantime.

data.title: Kinder Morgan: 2 Reasons Why Taking Profits Is Prudent Today (Downgrade)
data.description:
There was a great financial media hoopla about the S&P 500's 2nd bull market anniversary. Tech stocks attracted much of the attention given their massive run-up following the launch of ChatGPT in November 2022. But another niche has surprised many investors with relative strength, including a recent burst of upside.

The Global X MLP & Energy Infrastructure ETF (MLPX) has generally kept up with the SPX's torrid pace in the last 24 months. The pipeline stock fund is now near all-time highs on a total return basis amid volatility in the oil market. One of the ETF's biggest weights, Kinder Morgan (NYSE:KMI) is close to 52-week highs on its own and is up 47% in total return in 2024.

I am downgrading the company from a buy to a hold. Shares have returned 48% since my February buy rating. I now see the stock near fair value with a stretched technical chart. It reports Q3 results tonight.

Midstream Infrastructure ETF Soars 66% (Total Return) Last 2 Years

Back in July, KMI, the largest operator of carbon dioxide pipelines in North America, reported a mixed set of quarterly results. Q2 GAAP EPS of $0.26 beat the Wall Street consensus forecast by a penny. Revenue of $3.6 billion, up 2% from the same period a year earlier, was a significant $520 million miss. The management team approved a $0.2875 cash dividend and KMI had a distributable cash flow of $1.1 billion, just a modest sequential increase.

As of July, the firm sees budgeted net income attributable to KMI of $2.7 billion, or $1.22 per share, which would be a 15% increase from FY 2023. Total dividends are seen at $1.15 this year, a 2% potential rise.

Shares rose 2.5% in the session that followed. After the Q2 report, there have been more negative sell-side earnings revisions than positive, and energy prices have been volatile. Of course, this $55 billion market cap midstream stock is much less sensitive to ebbs and flows in oil and gas prices.

BofA recently upgraded shares from a hold to a buy, putting a new $27 price target on the stock. They cited potential long-term growth from Gulf Coast LNG projects and increased demand from AI and utility electrification and generally benefitting from new gas pipeline projects. The company has geographical diversification, which could help mitigate any post-election risks.

Looking ahead to tonight's earnings report, the options market has priced in a small 2.5% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle, expiring soonest after the release. That's about in line with the 2.3% average, per data from Option Research & Technology Services (ORATS). Seeking Alpha shows a consensus non-GAAP EPS figure of $0.27 on revenue of $4.05 billion.

Key risks for the company include weaker demand for LNG natural gas projects, unfavorable regulatory outcomes, particularly after the US elections, and higher interest rates that could result in a greater interest expense as KMI has $32 billion in total debt.

On the earnings outlook, analysts forecast a solid 12% rise in operating EPS this year, with more modest percentage gains in the out year and through 2026. Revenue, meanwhile, is seen increasing at a rate between 3% and 6%. There may be an upside to those broad outlooks if BofA's sanguine outlook comes to fruition - we may know more tonight. Free cash flow is not overly impressive at $0.94 over the past 12 months, though.

KMI: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revisions Trends

I see KMI as a solid company with a favorable outlook. On valuation, however, we run into a few stumbling blocks. The stock now features a forward non-GAAP price-to-earnings ratio of 20.6, more than four turns more expensive than its 5-year average.

I noted back in Q1 that the multiple was to the cheap side, but that can no longer be said. If we assume $1.26 of EPS in the next year and apply an 18 multiple, above its 5-year average, then the intrinsic value is just below $23. Shares also trade expensively on a price-to-sales basis.

With high share-price momentum, the stock could keep running, but I will note later that the technical situation is also stretched.

KMI: Stretched Valuation Metrics

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon shows a confirmed Q3 2024 earnings date of Wednesday, October 16 AMC with a conference call immediately after the results hit the tape.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With KMI shares modestly expensive after a stellar rally in 2024, the chart also shows some risks given the bullish fervor. Notice in the chart below that the stock is now about 40% above its long-term 200-day moving average. That's very extended for what's often a not-so-volatile stock. Moreover, if we take the previous trading range from the last few years - between $15.80 and $20.20, and add that on top of the breakout point of the low $20s, then the measured move price objective is very close to the recent high above $25.

KMI's RSI momentum oscillator is firmly in overbought conditions, while the 50-day moving average is way down under $22. That $22 spot is interesting in my eye, considering it's where KMI put in a short-term peak in July. I would be a technical buyer of the stock if it were to revisit $22. For now, though, the move appears extended, with a pullback possible into year-end.

KMI: Shares Reach Their Upside Price Objective, Eyeing $22 Support

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on KMI for two reasons. There's an emerging growth story with rising LNG demand and large infrastructure spending across the country. But with (1) a 20 P/E and (2) a near-parabolic chart, taking profits here is prudent.

data.title: FTSE 100: When Rates Drop, Stocks Pop
data.description:
Stocks Don't Always Pop

In theory, stock prices should increase as interest rates drop. This is due to three main reasons. The first being that lower rates usually disincentives consumers from saving cash and results in more spending instead, thereby leading to higher revenue for companies. The second is that the cost of borrowing also decreases. These two reasons alone would, therefore, have a positive impact on companies’ earnings, and subsequently, their share prices.

But perhaps most importantly, safer assets such as cash and government bonds begin to yield lower returns as rates drop. This then incentives investors to rotate into equities, as the opportunity cost of holding on to the former would be greater than the latter in a lower-rate environment. As such, it would only make sense to see a consistent trend of equities increasing in value when central banks cut rates, and the opposite when central banks raise rates.

Be that as it may, this isn’t always the case. According to research from Morningstar, US stocks have only had positive returns in two of the last four rate-cutting cycles in the 12 months after the first rate cut. This is because a rate-cutting cycle may come as a result of a recession, whose impact will have a greater effect on earnings than rate cuts will. This is why the Dot Com and housing market bubbles in 2001 and 2007 resulted in negative returns for US equities.

Nonetheless, it’s worth noting that the UK's headline index, the FTSE 100 (UKX), has seen an exception to this rule, as it rose by healthy double digits in 1990-1991 despite a recession. Still, the prevailing theory remains true – British stocks, on average, have done well when rate cuts are in motion, especially in the 12 months after the first rate cut.

Hence, with the slim likelihood of the UK entering a recession, the odds of UK equities going up in value over the next year are pretty decent – not just based on historical trends, but also based on their cheap valuations, and the potential for global investors to rotate into European equities for greater value.

British Bonanza

The sample size for the FTSE 100’s performance in past rate-cutting cycles isn’t enormous by any means. Nevertheless, it’s still decent enough to ascertain that UK stocks tend to do well after the first rate cut is instigated.

Our data shows that average returns tend to increase as the months go by. From an average of 2.2% within the first 3 months of a first rate cut, to 7.3% within 6 months, and 9.3% within 12 months – this matches the average return of the S&P 500 over the last 30 years. That said, it’s worth noting that these figures would be higher had it not been for the two instances where the Dot Com bubble burst and the Global Financial Crisis (GFC) occurred, in 2001 and 2008, respectively.

Even so, the trend that stocks go down during a recession isn’t always true with UK stocks, as mentioned earlier. That’s because in 1990, UK large-caps still experienced a boon, gaining an average of 22.9% in the 12 months after the first rate, despite having a sluggish start in the first 3 months.

However, when the UK avoids a recession/bubble-popping event during a rate-cutting cycle, returns are generally rather plum. These instances occurred in 1996-1997 and 1998-1999, where the average return was 31.5%. This came as lower borrowing costs paired with a higher risk appetite due to a lack of economic turmoil, generated favourable market conditions for risk assets to thrive.

Bank on Tech and Consumer Cyclicals?

So, which sectors perform best then? Well, including recessions and the Dot Com bubble, it’s no shock to see the technology sector outperforming by far, with a 1Y median average return of 37.1%.

This is down to one main reason – tech stocks tend to be growth stocks which are by nature, more risky than value plays. Therefore, in a lower interest rate environment, investors and traders alike are more willing to take on risk, especially when other assets like bonds are yielding lower returns. Hence why the likes of Relx (RELX), Sage (OTCPK:SGGEF), and WPP (WPP) outperformed during these periods.

The tech sector’s outperformance is followed closely by banks, which averaged a median 1Y return of 33.0%. This comes as no surprise, given that banks tend to go into distress when rates get jacked up. This is because although their income expands from higher interest, they are also likely to see those gains getting offset by much higher impairment charges. This was even more pronounced before liquidity and loan regulations instilled after the GFC.

But as rates decline, banks benefit. This is due to a strategy called structural hedging – when banks lock in higher yields by investing in long-term bonds which continue to generate high interest, despite paying less interest to customers as rates come down.

In addition to that, as impairment charges subside too, margins could be maintained as bad debts fell. Although, it's worth noting that this wasn’t the case during the GFC when bad debts skyrocketed, causing big banks like Lloyds (LYG), Barclays (BCS), NatWest (NWG), and many more to go into free fall.

Next on the list is consumer cyclicals, with a 1Y median return of 20.2%. This is also a sensible result, as cheaper borrowing cost usually sparks consumer sentiment, and consequently, consumer spending.

As most consumer cyclical stocks get less love during times of high interest as consumers rotate their spending into purchasing staples instead, a renewed feel-good factor that comes with rate cuts usually sparks a resurgence in sales for more discretionary items. As a result, the likes of AB Foods (OTCPK:ASBFY) and Kingfisher (OTCQX:KGFHF), amongst many, tend to see customers flock back to their stores.

Cyclical Performance

These underlying trends are even more pronounced when taking the exclusive sample in the data that excludes recessions (1996-1997 & 1998-1999) into account, as tech generated an average 1Y return of 76.1%, with banks not too far behind with a 60.2% return.

Having said that, consumer cyclical stocks don't see the same love when a recession doesn’t occur, only eking out an average 1Y return of 14.3%. This is probably due to the fact that the year-on-year growth in sales isn’t as pronounced, as consumers never had to ditch spending on discretionary items to begin with. Instead, telecommunication stocks took its place, outperforming tremendously in the mid-to-late 90s, with an average 1Y return of 57.5%.

History to Repeat Itself?

All this leads to the question of whether history can repeat itself. Can UK stocks outperform over the next year given that the Bank of England just triggered its first rate cut in August? Whilst there are parallels that can be drawn, there are also reasons as to why UK equities may not outperform as they have in the past despite not entering recession.

This is primarily due to a number of headwinds. These include the uncertainty surrounding the new Labour government’s tax and growth plans, a stock market that’s now heavily reliant on China’s growth, and consistent equity outflows from the UK stock market.

Moreover, it’s crucial to highlight that the global economy is completely different now than it was back then. Telecoms had a great run up in the late 90s as the internet had just broken ground then. But with artificial intelligence now the new kid on the block and the likes of BT (OTCPK:BTGOF) and Vodafone (VOD) laden with high levels of debt and lacklustre demand as compared to their prime days, earnings growth is likely to remain muted.

Meanwhile, mining and industrial stocks are now heavily reliant on China, which is currently going through its own set of economic challenges. Although the Chinese government’s recent monetary stimulus measures have propped up some of the miners' share prices from their lows, the medium-term sustainability of the appreciation in their appreciation remains ambiguous and on a knife’s edge, as a further rally will require more economic activity, and not just stimulus.

That being said, there are tailwinds that remain considerably stronger due to the cheap valuations that UK stocks have an offer as compared to their American counterparts. This is also why our base case remains that we expect UK stocks, especially large caps, to grow by mid-to-high single percentages over the next 12 months.

We anticipate there to be a continued recovery in bank stocks as well as housebuilders like Taylor Wimpey (OTCPK:TWODF) and Persimmon (OTCPK:PSMMF), which we see as the primary winners of the current rate-cutting cycle. We also see upside potential for consumer cyclicals, particularly in apparel and premium grocers like Marks and Spencer (OTCQX:MAKSF) and Ocado (OTCPK:OCDGF).

Alongside this, we see potential for the luxury sector to rebound, albeit heavily dependent on China. If this does happen, we think it will be back-loaded to the latter half of the 12-month window/in Q2 of 2025. In the event that it does, we expect this to be driven by a rebound in the Chinese economy, which would see miners and industrials rally in tandem as well.

To conclude, even though the response of UK stocks to rate cuts may not match the dramatic gains seen in some historical cycles, our overall outlook for the FTSE 100 remains cautiously positive. Because of this, investors should consider the unique current economic landscape and sector-specific opportunities available to them while they last. As Warren Buffett once said, “Price is what you pay; value is what you get", and that couldn't be more true today.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Jazz Pharmaceuticals: Unexpected Wins Improve The Long-Term Outlook
data.description:
Investors have shown little appreciation for two recent wins of Jazz Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:JAZZ), but I believe both improve the long-term outlook for the company. The first was a partial legal win against competitor Avadel Pharmaceuticals (AVDL) in late August, where Jazz was not able to get Avadel’s Lumryz off the narcolepsy market, but it was able to lock up the idiopathic hypersomnia market (though I should add that decisions are not final and that Avadel is appealing). The second win was this week's surprising positive outcome from the phase 3 trial of Zepzelca in combination with Tecentriq as maintenance therapy in patients with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer.

After the minor but expected pipeline setback that I covered in my previous article, it is good to see that there is some upside to my investment thesis as my assumption was that Zepzelca was more likely than not to fail in the mentioned trial, and my baseline assumption was that Jazz would not be able to stop Avadel from selling Lumryz in either indication.

Phase 3 win could open up a large market for Zepzelca

I was surprised to see the press release where Jazz said Zepzelca (lurbinectedin) in combination with Roche’s (OTCQX:RHHBY) Tecentriq (atezolizumab) achieved a statistically significant benefit on both endpoints of overall survival ('OS') and progression-free survival ('PFS') compared to Tecentriq monotherapy in patients with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer. My baseline assumption was that this trial would fail – there was no available clinical data supporting a successful outcome, and Zepzelca also failed in a confirmatory trial in the current second-line small cell lung cancer indication.

We have to wait to see the actual data before making estimates about the impact of the positive results with greater precision, but I believe it is safe to say that Zepzelca’s annual sales potential has more than doubled.

First, there are approximately 19,000 first-line ES-SCLC patients in the United States compared to 17,000 second-line patients that are eligible to receive Zepzelca monotherapy today.

Second, first-line patients are likely to stay on Zepzelca and Tecentriq longer than on Zepzelca monotherapy in the second-line setting, resulting in higher net sales per patient. For example, Zepzelca's median progression-free survival in second line SCLC is only 3.5 months, meaning patients stay on Zepzelca for a very short period of time since the drug is taken until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity.

It is reasonably likely that the progression-free survival in first-line maintenance setting in ES-SCLC patients is longer than 3.5 months. For example, Tecentriq achieved a PFS of 5.5 months in this population, and the phase 3 results of the combination with Zepzelca have reportedly extended both PFS and OS of Tecentriq. As such, I believe that PFS is at least in the 6-7-month range.

And third, Zepzelca showing an extension of PFS and OS as opposed to failing to show a treatment benefit in the confirmatory trial in second-line SCLC patients should lead to higher market penetration rates in this patient population.

Zepzelca generated $81 million in net sales in Q2 2024, a 15% increase over the same quarter last year, and I will wait for the presentation of efficacy and safety data from this trial to make a more precise estimate, but as a placeholder, I believe the potential approval of the Zepzelca and Tecentriq combination could add at least $400-500 million in Zepzelca annual sales by the end of the decade, and potentially significantly more if the progression-free survival and overall survival benefits are significantly better than what Zepzelca monotherapy was able to achieve in second-line small cell lung cancer.

Jazz expects to submit the sNDA for the combination of Zepzelca and Tecentriq in the first half of 2025, and the submission could receive Priority Review, which could shorten the review period to six months. If it does receive Priority Review, we could see Zepzelca approved for first-line maintenance in ES-SCLC patients before the end of 2025.

Partial legal win could lock Avadel’s Lumryz out of the idiopathic hypersomnia market

In late August, the United States District Court for the District of Delaware granted Jazz’s motion in part, preventing Avadel from seeking FDA approval or marketing of Lumryz for the treatment of idiopathic hypersomnia or “other indications not already part of Lumryz’s product labeling as of March 4, 2024, and enjoining Avadel from infringing in any way Claim 24 of the Company’s ‘782 patent by making, using or selling Lumryz through and including the February 2036 expiration date of the ‘782 patent.” The currently approved narcolepsy indication for Lumryz is excluded, and so are clinical trials ongoing as of the effective date of the injunction.

The Court also granted Jazz’s motion for an ongoing royalty on sales of Lumryz in the narcolepsy indication “at a rate to be determined based upon further briefing by the parties on the appropriate ongoing rate above 3.5%.”

I do not think either party is fully satisfied – Jazz sought to have Lumryz pulled from the market entirely, and Avadel sought to stay on the narcolepsy market and to expand to the idiopathic hypersomnia market.

As far as my investment thesis on Jazz is concerned, this is a clear win for Jazz because my baseline assumption was that the company would not be able to prevent Lumryz from being marketed for either narcolepsy or idiopathic hypersomnia. The latter market is particularly important since this is the indication Xyrem is not approved for and where Xywav should not be threatened by generic Xyrem (unlike the narcolepsy market where the authorized Xyrem generic is already having an impact on Jazz’s narcolepsy franchise). It is not easy to quantify the impact of this win, but keeping Lumryz out of the idiopathic hypersomnia market through 2036 likely preserves upwards of $1 billion in cumulative revenues for Jazz.

However, this may not be the final look of things for the two companies since Avadel is appealing this decision, and there is ongoing litigation between Jazz and the FDA because Jazz believes that the FDA violated the orphan drug exclusivity of Xywav by approving Lumryz for the treatment of narcolepsy.

Conclusion

Recent clinical and legal wins improve the long-term growth outlook for Jazz.

I believe Zepzelca’s phase 3 trial win in combination with Tecentriq represents an additional annual sales opportunity of at least $400-500 million a year for Jazz, and potentially more if the results (when they are presented) exceed my baseline assumptions.

On the legal side, the Court’s decisions on the dispute with Avadel put Jazz in a better position to defend Xywav’s market position in the idiopathic hypersomnia market and Jazz also gets to slightly benefit from Lumryz's narcolepsy sales in the form of a single-digit royalty rate. The caveat here is that the legal dispute is still far from over, and we could see additional changes after appeals and based on the outcomes of other ongoing trials.

The focus now turns back to quarterly sales updates where I do not expect significant deviations from the company’s full-year guidance or the Street consensus, and then, to the important phase 3 readout of zanidatamab in gastroesophageal adenocarcinoma (‘GEA’) patients in the second quarter of 2025. The results were pushed out by two quarters since this is an event-driven trial which needs to accrue enough disease progression events to complete. I have nothing new to add on this trial’s outcome to what I have already written – I expect Jazz to report positive results.
